text:
TitleAbstractIntroductionThis article explores the final pericope of the Book of Sirach as it has been handed down in the Greek version. This concluding section (Sir 51:13–30) consists of two chiasms that move in opposite directions structurally: an autobiographical section (vv. 13–22) describing the Author’s lifelong journey in pursuit of Wisdom, and a call to instruction (vv. 23–30) addressed by the Author to the uninstructed, inviting them to enroll in his school.

Wisdom here is understood in a thoroughly religious sense as Prayer and the moral fruit thereof. Although the author recommends that the search for Wisdom start from an early age, it demands the totality of man’s existence and therefore all areas of human life must be sacrificed to this pursuit. This section explains in more detail the essential characteristics of the path that leads to Wisdom: purification, the observation of the Torah and the walks in the ways of integrity. The reward given to one who searches for Wisdom are “good possessions” — joy, discernment and a tongue whereby to render praise to the Lord, as well as to teach others.

One of the dominant ideas in the text is the sage’s accessibility. This concept is markedly different from the seemingly impenetrable fortress of pessimism encountered in Job and Ecclesiastes. The underlying message in Sirach is that that Wisdom is attainable as long as one remains steadfast in their search. The text in question elucidates two ways to acquire knowledge: by seeking through personal means, or by learning from the teacher’s prior knowledge. This passage may be seen as crucial in summing up the theology of Ben Sira, as it serves as a literary “book-end” together with the prologue (1:1–10). It harmoniously integrates all the key ideas found within the book of Ben Sira, showcasing the underlying optimism within the text. This central message pertains to the notion that humans can truly attain a connection with God.
explanation: The text reads as a concise scholarly analysis with nuanced interpretation of Sirach, suggesting human authorship; its logical flow and specific references imply deliberate scholarly craft, though polished AI could also produce similar prose.
